threats.title: changeBridgedToken Deletes Wrong Mapping Key Leaving Stale Canonical Reference
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken, the code executes `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` which is a no-op since _btokenNew was verified to have no existing mapping (bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr == address(0)). The intended deletion should be `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` to clean up the old bridged token's canonical mapping. After migration, btokenOld_ retains its bridgedToCanonical mapping despite being blacklisted.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect. The stale mapping means bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] still maps to the canonical token, breaking the bijection invariant between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged. While the blacklist prevents sendToken usage, the stale data persists in storage and could cause issues in future upgrades or off-chain systems.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] is no-op; should be delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

preconditions: Owner calls changeBridgedToken with existing old bridged token
btokenOld_ != address(0)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists with stale canonical token data after migration
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No post-condition check that old mapping was cleared

analysis_questions: Can the stale mapping be exploited to bypass blacklist in any code path?
Does _handleMessage check blacklist before or after checking bridgedToCanonical?
Could a future upgrade reference stale bridgedToCanonical data?
validation_requirements: Verify bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists after changeBridgedToken
Check all code paths that read bridgedToCanonical for blacklist bypass potential
Confirm storage inconsistency between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner migrates token. Old token retains canonical mapping. If blacklist check is ever bypassed or removed in upgrade, old token could be used to burn and trigger transfers based on stale mapping.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Storage inconsistency; potential double-accounting if blacklist is bypassed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the migrated token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to clean stale mapping

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] cleanup after migration

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Bug triggered on every changeBridgedToken call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Verifiable via storage read after changeBridgedToken

threats.title: Bridge Message with Short Data Bypasses Selector Check Allowing Arbitrary Fallback Invocation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _invokeMessageCall, the selector restriction only applies when _message.data.length >= 4 AND _message.to.isContract(). If data.length is 1-3 bytes and the target is a contract, the call proceeds to excessivelySafeCall with the short data. This triggers the target contract's fallback function with arbitrary short payloads while carrying _message.value in ETH and having bridge context active.
threats.investigation_rationale: The selector check exists to restrict contract calls to only onMessageInvocation. The bypass via short data (<4 bytes) allows invoking fallback functions on arbitrary contract targets through the bridge, potentially confusing contracts that check bridge context.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _message.data.length >= 4 check means <4 bytes bypass selector restriction entirely

preconditions: Attacker sends bridge message with data.length between 1 and 3
Target is a contract with a fallback or receive function
Target is not bridge, signal_service, address(0), or banned

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Short data (<4 bytes) passes through the else branch to excessivelySafeCall without selector validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check for data.length > 0 && data.length < 4 case with contract targets

analysis_questions: Are there any deployed contracts that use Bridge.context() in their fallback?
Can the bridge context during fallback invocation be exploited by any known contract?
What is the practical impact of invoking fallback with short data + ETH?
validation_requirements: Send bridge message with 1-3 byte data to a contract target
Verify fallback is invoked with bridge context active
Check if any protocol contracts have exploitable fallback functions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker crafts message with 1-3 byte data targeting a contract. The selector check is bypassed, and the contract's fallback executes within the bridge context. If the target reads bridge context, it may process the call as a legitimate cross-chain message.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Depends on target contract's fallback behavior
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of contracts with fallback functions reading bridge context

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for short-data message to contract targets

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring messages with short data targeting contracts

threats.title: SignalService Cannot Be Paused Leaving No Emergency Stop During Active Exploit
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService._authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, making the pause() and unpause() functions permanently inoperative. If a vulnerability is discovered in Merkle proof verification (LibTrieProof, MerkleTrie) or in the signal storage mechanism, there is no way to halt SignalService operations. Since Bridge._proveSignalReceived depends on SignalService for proof verification, all bridge operations continue even during an active exploit of the proof system.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the bridge itself can be paused via bridge_pauser, the SignalService cannot. An attacker exploiting a proof verification bug could continue proving signals even if the bridge is paused, potentially pre-positioning proofs for immediate execution when the bridge is unpaused.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

preconditions: A vulnerability exists in proof verification logic
Emergency response requires halting proof operations

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _authorizePause always reverts, making pause permanently unavailable
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No emergency stop mechanism for signal/proof operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative halt mechanism for SignalService

analysis_questions: Can pausing the bridge alone provide sufficient protection?
Can proveSignalReceived with caching continue to write state when bridge is paused?
Is there an alternative way to disable SignalService (e.g., deauthorize all addresses)?
validation_requirements: Confirm pause() on SignalService always reverts
Verify proveSignalReceived can still cache chain data when bridge is paused
Assess residual risk if only bridge is paused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Proof verification bug discovered. Bridge is paused, but SignalService continues accepting proof operations. Attacker caches malicious chain data via proveSignalReceived. When bridge is unpaused, cached data enables immediate exploitation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to halt proof operations during exploit window

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying SignalService emergency pause behavior

threats.title: Unchecked Invocation Delay Addition Can Overflow to Bypass Security Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: In processMessage, when msg.sender is not the preferredExecutor, the invocationDelay is increased by invocationExtraDelay inside an unchecked block: `unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; }`. While current hardcoded values (3600 + 384) are safe, getInvocationDelays() is a virtual function that can be overridden in derived contracts. If a derived Bridge contract returns large delay values, the unchecked addition wraps around to a small value, effectively eliminating the security delay.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked addition of two uint256 values in a security-critical path creates a potential overflow if delay values are ever increased significantly in a derived contract or future upgrade.
deployment_assumptions: getInvocationDelays is overridden in a derived contract with large values
threats.config_evidence: Bridge.sol getInvocationDelays is virtual; current values are safe but future overrides may not be

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; } - uint256 addition in unchecked block

preconditions: getInvocationDelays returns large values that cause overflow when summed
Derived contract or upgrade changes delay values

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If invocationDelay + invocationExtraDelay > type(uint256).max, result wraps to small value, reducing security delay
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No overflow check on the addition; no upper bound validation on delay values

analysis_questions: Are there any deployed derived Bridge contracts with overridden getInvocationDelays?
Could a future upgrade introduce delay values large enough to overflow?
What is the minimum pair of values that would cause an exploitable overflow?
validation_requirements: Test with maxed delay values to demonstrate overflow
Verify overflow results in bypassed delay check

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Derived Bridge contract or future upgrade sets getInvocationDelays to return values where sum overflows. Non-preferred executor calls processMessage; the overflow results in near-zero effective delay, bypassing the security window.
prerequisites: Derived contract with overridden getInvocationDelays returning large values

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete bypass of invocation delay security window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All bridge users on the affected chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fix delay values via contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: No overflow test for invocation delay addition

threats.title: Unauthorized Chain Data Caching via Direct proveSignalReceived Calls Bypasses syncChainData Authorization
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService.syncChainData requires isAuthorized[msg.sender]==true. However, proveSignalReceived is public and calls _cacheChainData internally, which calls _syncChainData. This bypasses the authorization check, allowing any caller to write chain data (state roots, signal roots) and advance topBlockId for any chain. While the data must be validated by Merkle proofs, the authorization restriction on syncChainData is rendered meaningless for data that can be proven via multi-hop proofs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The authorization gate on syncChainData suggests the protocol wants to control who can write chain data. The caching path through proveSignalReceived circumvents this control. An attacker could advance topBlockId to recent non-finalized blocks, potentially affecting getSyncedChainData results used by other protocol components.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proveSignalReceived calls _cacheChainData → _syncChainData without isAuthorized check; syncChainData requires isAuthorized

preconditions: Caller provides valid multi-hop Merkle proof with non-zero CacheOption
proveSignalReceived is called directly (not via Bridge's staticcall)
Valid chain data exists to prove against

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: SignalService.sol lines 120-130 (_cacheChainData call) and 227-248 (_cacheChainData writes via _syncChainData)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can write chain data and advance topBlockId by providing valid proofs with cache options
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No authorization check in the caching path; no restriction on CacheOption values

analysis_questions: Can advancing topBlockId to non-finalized blocks cause issues?
Do any protocol components rely on topBlockId for security decisions?
Can this be used for storage griefing (filling slots)?
Note: Bridge uses staticcall to proveSignalReceived preventing caching through bridge path
validation_requirements: Call proveSignalReceived directly with CACHE_BOTH option
Verify chain data is written and topBlockId is advanced
Confirm syncChainData authorization is bypassed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls SignalService.proveSignalReceived directly with valid multi-hop proof and CacheOption.CACHE_BOTH. Chain data is cached and topBlockId is advanced without going through the authorized syncChainData path.
prerequisites: Valid Merkle proof available for the target chain data
Direct call to SignalService (not through bridge)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited direct impact; topBlockId advancement could affect downstream consumers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol components relying on topBlockId or cached chain data

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for unauthorized caching via proveSignalReceived

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Standard proof verification gas + caching writes
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable: ChainDataSynced events from non-authorized addresses

threats.title: Bridge.sendMessage may succeed while SignalService.sendSignal silently fails (no return-value check), breaking proofability and locking funds
threats.hypothesis_description: Bridge.sendMessage performs an external call to ISignalService.sendSignal(msgHash_) but does not check a boolean success flag (it is a standard interface call). If resolve("signal_service") points to a contract that returns success but fails to persist the signal (or a misconfigured non-compliant contract that reverts selectively), the bridge may emit MessageSent and accept ETH but the message becomes unprovable on destination chains, potentially locking value/fees until recall paths (which may also require signals).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration defect risk: the bridge’s core correctness depends on SignalService behavior, but Bridge does not assert any post-condition (e.g., isSignalSent) after sending. Because this protocol relies on AddressResolver wiring, misconfiguration or an upgraded SignalService implementation bug can cause permanent message loss.
deployment_assumptions: AddressResolver can be configured/upgraded such that "signal_service" points to a non-conforming or buggy implementation
SignalService implementation could be upgraded to one that does not persist signals as expected
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol shows UUPS upgradeable deployments and later upgrades via timelock; AddressManager can be updated (SetAddress.s.sol / SetRemoteBridgeSuites.s.sol). No explicit post-send verification in Bridge.sendMessage.

proof_of_signal.lines: sendMessage() external call to ISignalService(...).sendSignal(msgHash_); no subsequent assertion
proof_of_signal.pattern: external dependency call without verifying effect (no isSignalSent check / no try-catch guarding semantics)

preconditions: Signal service address returned by resolve("signal_service") is incorrect/buggy/malicious (via config or upgrade)
Bridge.sendMessage accepts ETH and emits MessageSent even though signal not persisted
Destination relies on proofs of the signal to process/recall

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no verification of external dependency side effects
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: message becomes unprovable and funds stuck

analysis_questions: Can SignalService.sendSignal be made to not persist while not reverting (by upgrade/malicious impl)?
Would such a failure strand funds in Bridge (no process, no recall) given the protocol flow?
validation_requirements: Simulate a signal_service that returns successfully but does not write storage, and observe bridge behavior
Check whether recallMessage requires the signal to be sent (it does via isSignalSent check) and whether that blocks recovery

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If signal_service is misconfigured/upgraded to a noncompliant contract, user sends message with value+fee; Bridge accepts and emits MessageSent but no signal exists. Destination cannot prove receipt; source recallMessage also fails because isSignalSent is false; ETH becomes trapped in Bridge.
prerequisites: Configuration error or malicious/buggy upgrade of signal_service

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential permanent lock of message value+fee in Bridge
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All bridge users during misconfiguration window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention/upgrade; users cannot self-recover if signal never existed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests that assert sendMessage implies isSignalSent==true

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor mismatch between MessageSent events and SignalService.isSignalSent state

threats.title: Bridge context validity depends on chainid-specific transient storage; any EVM incompatibility on L1 could break vault authorization or context reset
threats.hypothesis_description: Bridge uses EIP-1153 transient storage (tstore/tload) for context and reentrancy lock only when block.chainid == 1. If deployed to an L1-like environment that does not support transient storage semantics as expected (or if execution clients have a bug), Bridge.context() and nonReentrant may malfunction: context could appear invalid to vaults (breaking bridging) or fail to reset properly across internal calls.
threats.investigation_rationale: Given foundry.toml targets Cancun, transient storage is expected; however, correctness critically depends on chain feature availability. Any mismatch yields chain-wide DoS of message processing/recall due to VAULT_PERMISSION_DENIED or B_INVALID_CONTEXT. This is a practical operational threat on alternative deployments/forks.
deployment_assumptions: Bridge is deployed on a chain where block.chainid == 1 but transient storage is not available/behaves unexpectedly (private fork, misconfigured EVM, pre-Cancun L1 simulation)
Or a client bug causes tstore/tload to persist unexpectedly within tx
threats.config_evidence: EssentialContract and Bridge both branch on block.chainid==1 to use tstore/tload. foundry.toml sets evm_version=cancun, but production client support is a deployment assumption.

proof_of_signal.pattern: chainid-dependent transient storage use for correctness-critical authorization context

preconditions: Bridge deployed/executed in an environment where tstore/tload is unsupported or buggy
Vaults rely on Bridge.context() for authorization checks (BaseVault.checkProcessMessageContext)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: context invalid -> vault callbacks revert -> messages become RETRIABLE/FAILED unexpectedly or unprocessable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no runtime feature detection; assumes chainid==1 implies transient storage works

analysis_questions: Is this code ever deployed on chainid==1 in environments without Cancun?
Can context leak or read as zero/placeholder causing B_INVALID_CONTEXT/VAULT_PERMISSION_DENIED in normal flows?
validation_requirements: Run integration tests on pre-Cancun EVM settings to see if Bridge breaks
Confirm that all L1 deployments are indeed Cancun-capable (or chainid not 1)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not attacker-driven; an infrastructure/EVM mismatch causes Bridge.context() to revert for vault calls, effectively halting cross-chain token releases/mints.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bridged assets stuck due to failed message invocation/recall
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All bridge users on affected deployment

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely lacks tests across multiple EVM versions for chainid==1 path

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: watch for systematic B_INVALID_CONTEXT/REENTRANT_CALL anomalies on L1

threats.title: Relayer Gas Griefing Fee Theft via Insufficient Gas in processMessage
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with deliberately insufficient gas. The EVM's 63/64 rule causes excessivelySafeCall to forward less gas than _message.gasLimit to the target, causing the invocation to fail. The message is marked RETRIABLE and the relayer pockets the entire fee, while the user must pay gas again to retry with no fee incentive for retriers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code does not validate that gasleft() is sufficient to forward _message.gasLimit before calling _invokeMessageCall. On L2 (invocationDelay=0), proof and execution happen atomically in one transaction, giving the relayer full control. This is exploitable by any address without special privileges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasLimit = msg.sender == _message.destOwner ? gasleft() : _message.gasLimit; No check that gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before forwarding to excessivelySafeCall

preconditions: Message with non-zero fee exists and is proven on destination chain
Relayer is not the destOwner (uses _message.gasLimit path)
On L2 chain (invocationDelay=0) for single-tx atomic exploitation
Message gasLimit is significantly higher than tx overhead gas cost

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: gasLimit = msg.sender == _message.destOwner ? gasleft() : _message.gasLimit
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer provides tx gas = overhead + small buffer; EVM 63/64 rule gives call min(gasLimit, 63/64 * gasleft) which is far below _message.gasLimit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer profit = message.fee - tx_gas_cost. For messages with reasonable fees (>0.01 ETH) and low gas overhead (~200k gas), profit margin is significant

economic_questions: What is the typical fee-to-gas-cost ratio for bridge messages?
How many RETRIABLE messages exist that were never retried?
Is there a minimum fee enforced at the application layer?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: processMessage with gas = overhead + 50k, verify message becomes RETRIABLE
Measure exact gas overhead for processMessage excluding the external call
Calculate break-even fee amount for griefing attack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.01 ETH gas cost for tx submission
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: message.fee minus gas cost per message
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fee; potentially 50-90% of fee value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time messages with fees are pending on L2
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, pure fee extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who send bridge messages with non-zero fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of message fee + cost to retry
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate of all pending message fees

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: gasLimit values in typical bridge messages, fee amounts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify 63/64 rule causes silent failure for external call

threats.title: Bridge staticcall Prevents SignalService Proof Caching, Causing Valid Proofs to Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: Bridge._proveSignalReceived uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. However, proveSignalReceived may call _syncChainData (which performs SSTORE) when CacheOption is non-zero. SSTORE in a staticcall context causes the entire staticcall to revert, making the proof appear invalid. This means any proof submitted with cache options through Bridge will be silently rejected.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has a fundamental mismatch: SignalService.proveSignalReceived is designed to optionally cache chain data (state modifications), but Bridge calls it via staticcall (which prohibits state modifications). Proofs with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING will always fail when verified through Bridge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _signalService.staticcall(data) where data encodes proveSignalReceived which may SSTORE via _cacheChainData

preconditions: Relayer constructs HopProof with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING
The proof path includes a hop with cacheStateRoot or cacheSignalRoot conditions met
Bridge calls _proveSignalReceived which uses staticcall

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: staticcall + SSTORE = revert; all non-CACHE_NOTHING proofs fail through Bridge
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayers unaware of this limitation submit proofs with caching, which fail silently. Messages appear unprocessable.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS vector: attacker could confuse relayers or block specific message processing if relayers use caching by default

economic_questions: Do existing relayer implementations default to CACHE_NOTHING?
Can an attacker construct a proof that FORCES caching via specific hop conditions?
Is the caching feature intended to be used only via direct SignalService calls?
validation_requirements: Submit processMessage with a proof having CACHE_BOTH and verify it reverts with B_NOT_RECEIVED
Verify same proof succeeds with CACHE_NOTHING
Check if any non-last-hop condition forces _syncChainData even with CACHE_NOTHING

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; DoS impact on message processing
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent until relayers learn to use CACHE_NOTHING

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could delay bridge message processing

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose messages are processed with caching proofs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Message stuck until re-proven with CACHE_NOTHING
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All messages attempted with non-zero CacheOption

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test CacheOption values through Bridge's staticcall path
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm SSTORE in staticcall causes revert

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Drains ERC20Vault on Destination Chain
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault._handleMessage on the source chain correctly accounts for fee-on-transfer tokens via balance delta measurement. However, _transferTokens on the destination chain calls `IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount)` with the full recorded amount. If the canonical token on the destination chain has transfer fees, the recipient receives less than _amount but the vault's accounting considers the full amount transferred. Over multiple bridge cycles, the vault's canonical token balance becomes insufficient to back all bridged tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Source-side accounting uses balance delta (correct), but destination-side assumes full transfer (incorrect for fee-on-transfer tokens). This asymmetry creates a slow drain of canonical token reserves from the destination vault.
deployment_assumptions: Canonical ERC20 token on destination chain has transfer fees (fee-on-transfer behavior)
threats.config_evidence: ERC20Vault.sol:_handleMessage lines 300-314 measures balance delta; _transferTokens line 266 does safeTransfer(_to, _amount) without delta check. No deployment evidence of specific fee-on-transfer tokens being used.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount) transfers full amount without measuring actual balance change on destination

preconditions: Canonical token on destination chain is a fee-on-transfer token
Token is bridged across chains where destination holds canonical tokens
Multiple bridge operations occur accumulating the deficit

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeTransfer(_to, _amount) where actual transfer = _amount - fee; vault balance decreases by _amount but recipient gets less
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker repeatedly bridges fee-on-transfer token back and forth, each cycle draining vault by the fee amount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle drains fee_percentage * amount from vault. After N cycles, vault becomes insolvent.

What is the cumulative deficit per cycle?

threats.title: ERC1155Vault Uses Unchecked Block Around External Calls in _handleMessage
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC1155Vault._handleMessage wraps the entire token handling logic in an `unchecked` block, including external calls to BridgedERC1155.burn() and IERC1155.safeTransferFrom(). While the loop counter overflow is protected by MAX_TOKEN_PER_TXN=10, the unchecked block also applies to any arithmetic within the external call context setup. If future modifications add arithmetic within this unchecked scope, overflow/underflow protection is silently disabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked block is overly broad - it should only wrap the loop increment, not the entire function body including external calls. This is a code quality issue that creates latent risk for future modifications. The same pattern exists in ERC721Vault._handleMessage.

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Bridged Assets via Push-over-Pull ETH Refund DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The Bridge and Vault contracts use a push-over-pull pattern for ETH refunds and asset recalls. If the refund recipient (refundTo) or source owner is a smart contract that reverts on receiving ETH, the entire transaction reverts, permanently locking the bridged assets in the NEW state.
threats.investigation_rationale: Pushing ETH to arbitrary addresses is a known anti-pattern. Since processMessage reverts entirely if the refund fails, the message can never reach the FAILED state, preventing the user from recalling their assets on the source chain. This represents a permanent loss of funds for users bridging to smart contract wallets (like Gnosis Safe) that lack a payable fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: refundTo.sendEther(_message.fee + refundAmount) pushes ETH and reverts on failure

preconditions: User bridges assets to a destination address or specifies a refundTo address that is a smart contract
The target contract reverts on receiving ETH (e.g., missing receive function or out of gas)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: refundTo.sendEther() reverts the entire processMessage transaction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Message status remains NEW forever because processMessage cannot complete
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but results in 100% loss of bridged assets for the victim due to permanent lock

economic_questions: Can a user intentionally grief themselves to lock funds?
Are there any fallback mechanisms to recover stuck NEW messages?
validation_requirements: Test processMessage with a reverting refundTo contract

threats.title: Global Reentrancy Lock Prevents Cross-Chain Contract Composability
threats.hypothesis_description: Both Bridge.sendMessage and Bridge.processMessage use the nonReentrant modifier, which relies on a single contract-level lock. If the target contract attempts to send a message back (e.g., returning excess funds or acknowledging receipt) by calling Bridge.sendMessage, the call will revert with REENTRANT_CALL.
threats.investigation_rationale: This architectural flaw severely limits cross-chain composability. Any smart contract on the destination chain that automatically triggers a return bridge message upon receiving a cross-chain call will deterministically fail. This breaks standard DeFi patterns like cross-chain ping-pong, atomic cross-chain swaps, and automated yield harvesting.

threats.title: Bridge retryMessage Gas Griefing Prevents Successful Message Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address can call retryMessage with _isLastAttempt=false for any RETRIABLE message. The function uses gasleft() as gasLimit. threats.title: BridgedERC20Base Burn-Before-Mint Ordering Creates Temporary Double Token Supply During Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: In BridgedERC20Base.burn() during outbound migration, IBridgedERC20(migratingAddress).mint(_account, _amount) is called BEFORE _burnToken(_account, _amount). threats.title: ERC20Vault onMessageRecalled Token-ETH Coupling Causes Permanent Fund Lock

threats.title: ERC721Vault onMessageInvocation _safeMint Callback During Bridge Context Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: When BridgedERC721 tokens are minted during ERC721Vault.onMessageInvocation, _safeMint is used which triggers an onERC721Received callback on the recipient. This callback executes while the Bridge context is still set (msgHash, from, srcChainId). Although nonReentrant guards protect both Bridge and Vault from re-entry, the recipient contract receives execution with the bridge context readable via Bridge.context(). The recipient could use this callback to interact with external protocols that query bridge context for authorization decisions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _safeMint callback grants execution to the recipient during bridge message processing. While direct re-entry to bridge/vault is blocked, the recipient can call any other contract. If any external protocol uses Bridge.context() to make authorization decisions, the callback could be exploited to impersonate the bridge's cross-chain message context.

threats.title: ERC1155Vault onMessageRecalled safeBatchTransferFrom Callback Creates Execution Window During Recall

During this callback, the bridge context shows ctx.from==bridge (from checkRecallMessageContext), and the vault's reentrancy lock is held. threats.title: Bridge sendMessage Allows Zero gasLimit Messages That Can Only Be Processed by destOwner
threats.hypothesis_description: Bridge.sendMessage does not validate that gasLimit > 0. Messages with gasLimit=0 can only be processed by destOwner (processMessage enforces msg.sender==destOwner for gasLimit=0). If destOwner is set to an address that cannot call processMessage (e.g., a contract without this capability, or an EOA that loses access), the message is permanently stuck. The ETH (value+fee) is locked in the bridge forever since the message can never reach FAILED status (only RETRIABLE→FAILED, but it's stuck at NEW).
threats.investigation_rationale: sendMessage has no minimum gasLimit check. A user could accidentally or maliciously create messages that can only be processed by a specific address. If that address is unavailable, the message and its ETH are permanently locked. This particularly impacts bridge value when used programmatically by contracts that set destOwner to their own address but don't implement processMessage capabilities.

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit; permanent fund lock for the user

threats.title: ERC1155Vault sendToken Encodes Amounts in Message But No Verification on Destination of Array Consistency

threats.title: Suspend+Unsuspend Creates Proof Receipt for Arbitrary Message Hashes Bypassing Merkle Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: suspendMessages sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt to type(uint64).max (suspend) or block.timestamp (unsuspend). In processMessage, the check `bool isMessageProven = receivedAt != 0` skips _proveSignalReceived entirely if a proof receipt exists. A compromised bridge_watchdog can: (1) call suspendMessages([crafted_hash], true), (2) call suspendMessages([crafted_hash], false) which sets receivedAt=block.timestamp, (3) wait for invocation delay, (4) process a fabricated message matching the hash without any Merkle proof verification. Unlike recallMessage which checks isSignalSent, processMessage has no such additional check.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical design flaw in the suspend/unsuspend mechanism. The unsuspend action creates a proof receipt without proof verification. Combined with the default NEW(0) message status for all hashes, any crafted message matching an unsuspended hash can be processed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: suspendMessages sets receivedAt=block.timestamp on unsuspend; processMessage skips proof if receivedAt != 0

preconditions: bridge_watchdog key is compromised or colluding

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsuspend sets receivedAt=block.timestamp for any msgHash; processMessage checks only receivedAt != 0 to skip proof

threats.title: Bridge Watchdog Can Indefinitely Freeze User Funds via Message Suspension Without Escape Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: The bridge_watchdog can suspend any message by setting proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = type(uint64).max via suspendMessages. Suspended messages cannot be processed because block.timestamp will never reach invocationDelay + type(uint64).max. threats.title: Stale bridgedToCanonical entry due to wrong deletion in ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken can re-enable old bridged token in future code paths and break canonical↔bridged bijection

This leaves stale canonical metadata on the old bridged token forever. While btokenOld_ is blacklisted for sendToken, other code paths (future upgrades, off-chain systems, or direct uses of bridgedToCanonical) may treat the old token as canonical-backed, enabling confusion or potential mint/burn routing errors.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect (wrong key deletion) flagged by Phase A invariants. Even if not immediately exploitable today due to blacklist checks in sendToken, it is a high-value correctness issue that can become exploitable after upgrades or via any missing blacklist check elsewhere.

threats.title: SignalService.sendSignal allows anyone to write arbitrary signal slots, enabling state bloat and potentially increased proof costs (storage-grief)
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService.sendSignal is permissionless and stores _signal as value in a deterministic slot derived from (chainid, msg.sender, _signal). An attacker can spam sendSignal with many unique signals, permanently increasing contract storage and potentially increasing state sync overhead or archive-node costs. threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical chain-level grief risk; SignalService is unpausable by design and is a shared dependency. Even if storage writes are paid by attacker, the long-term operational burden is borne by node operators and could affect protocol reliability.

threats.title: Vaults may become irrecoverably stuck when transferring canonical NFTs to contracts that reject ERC721/ERC1155 receipts during onMessageInvocation/onMessageRecalled
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC721Vault and ERC1155Vault use safeTransferFrom/safeBatchTransferFrom when releasing canonical NFTs to the recipient or returning them on recall. If the recipient (to or srcOwner) is a contract that does not implement the receiver interfaces correctly, the transfer reverts, causing the entire bridge message execution/recall to revert. This can strand messages in NEW/RETRIABLE and lock assets in the vault indefinitely (since the bridge uses push-style delivery).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a common but practical failure mode for NFT bridges because recipients are often contracts (multisigs, vaults). Even without an attacker, it creates user-funds lock risk; with an attacker, a malicious recipient contract can intentionally brick withdrawals to force operational intervention.

threats.title: Suspend/Unsuspend Bypasses Proof Verification Enabling Fabricated Message Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: A compromised bridge_watchdog can call suspendMessages(hashes, true) then suspendMessages(hashes, false) for any arbitrary msgHash. The unsuspend sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = block.timestamp. In processMessage, receivedAt != 0 causes isMessageProven = true, completely SKIPPING Merkle proof verification via _proveSignalReceived. Since messageStatus defaults to NEW (0) for all hashes, ANY crafted message matching the suspended-then-unsuspended hash can be executed without cross-chain proof. Unlike recallMessage (which has isSignalSent check), processMessage has NO secondary verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the most critical code-level vulnerability identified. processMessage's only defense against fabricated messages is _proveSignalReceived, and the suspend/unsuspend mechanism creates a path to bypass it entirely. The asymmetry with recallMessage (which has isSignalSent check) confirms this is unintentional.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: bridge_watchdog is not registered in deployment scripts, meaning only owner can call suspendMessages. But AddressManager can be updated post-deployment to add a bridge_watchdog address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: suspendMessages sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = uint64(block.timestamp) on unsuspend; processMessage lines 188-191 check receivedAt != 0 to skip proof

preconditions: Attacker has compromised bridge_watchdog key (or owner key)
Bridge holds ETH or vaults hold tokens to be drained
Attacker can compute message hash for crafted message

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = uint64(block.timestamp) WITHOUT any proof verification
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Craft message M, compute H=hashMessage(M), suspend+unsuspend H, wait invocationDelay, call processMessage(M, emptyProof)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Drain entire Bridge ETH balance + trigger arbitrary vault operations (mint bridged tokens, release canonical tokens)

threats.title: Unchecked Overflow in invocationDelay Addition Could Bypass Security Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: In processMessage, the code uses `unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; }` when the caller is not the preferredExecutor. Since getInvocationDelays is a virtual function that can be overridden in derived contracts, a custom implementation returning large uint256 values could cause this unchecked addition to wrap around to a small number, effectively eliminating the security delay on L1.
threats.investigation_rationale: While current hardcoded values (3600 + 384) are safe, the virtual function pattern combined with unchecked arithmetic creates a latent vulnerability. Any derived Bridge contract overriding getInvocationDelays with values approaching uint256.max would break the time-based security mechanism.
threats.config_evidence: Bridge.sol getInvocationDelays is declared virtual at line 267. Current values are hardcoded and safe.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; } where both values come from virtual function

threats.title: SignalService Cannot Be Paused During Active Exploit Due to _authorizePause Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService._authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, making the SignalService permanently unpausable. If a vulnerability is discovered in Merkle proof verification (LibTrieProof/MerkleTrie) or in the multi-hop proof logic, there is no way to halt proof processing. All bridge operations that depend on proof verification continue to be exploitable even during an active attack.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike Bridge and Vaults which can be paused via bridge_pauser, SignalService has no emergency stop. Since all cross-chain proof verification flows through SignalService.proveSignalReceived (called by Bridge via staticcall), an unpausable SignalService means the entire proof layer cannot be shut down.
threats.config_evidence: SignalService.sol line 194: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

proof_of_signal.pattern: _authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, preventing any pause

threats.title: Merkle Proof Bypass via Watchdog Suspend/Unsuspend
threats.hypothesis_description: Unsuspending a message sets `receivedAt` to `block.timestamp`. If a never-proven message is suspended and unsuspended, it bypasses `_proveSignalReceived` entirely, allowing execution of forged messages.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `suspendMessages` function does not verify if a message was previously proven before setting `proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt`. When unsuspended, `receivedAt` becomes non-zero, tricking `processMessage` into believing the message was already proven via Merkle proof.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = _timestamp; without checking prior state

preconditions: bridge_watchdog role is compromised or acts maliciously
Watchdog suspends and unsuspends a fabricated message hash

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows unproven messages to be processed
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Require receivedAt != 0 before allowing suspend, or track proven status separately

analysis_questions: Can a compromised watchdog drain all bridge funds using this bypass?
Is there any secondary validation that prevents the message from executing?
validation_requirements: Create a fake message hash
Call suspendMessages(true) then suspendMessages(false) on it
Call processMessage and verify it executes without a valid proof

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker compromises the `bridge_watchdog` key. They craft a fake message transferring all bridge ETH to themselves. They compute the hash, call `suspendMessages` (true then false) on it. They wait for `invocationDelay`, then call `processMessage`. The bridge skips the Merkle proof check and sends the ETH.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of bridge and vault assets

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for processing unproven but unsuspended messages

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable as suspend/unsuspend followed by unproven execution

threats.title: BridgedERC20.setSnapshoter allows setting snapshooter to arbitrary address, enabling governance snapshot manipulation (if used)
threats.hypothesis_description: Owner can set `snapshooter` to any address, which can then call snapshot(). If downstream governance or airdrops rely on snapshots for voting power or allocations, a compromised owner or misconfiguration could enable arbitrary snapshot timing that benefits an attacker (e.g., snapshot right after mint/bridge-in).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a role-risk rather than an access bypass (works as designed), but it can be security-relevant if snapshots influence token distribution or governance outcomes. Worth validating intended operational controls and whether snapshooter should be constrained (e.g., timelock).
deployment_assumptions: BridgedERC20 snapshots are used for governance/airdrops with economic consequences
Owner key or timelock governance can be compromised/misused
threats.config_evidence: BridgedERC20.sol exposes setSnapshoter onlyOwner with no constraints; no deployment evidence of snapshooter policy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner can delegate snapshot power to arbitrary EOA/contract

preconditions: BridgedERC20 is used in a system where snapshots affect allocations/voting
Owner sets snapshooter to attacker-controlled address (or attacker compromises owner/governance)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unrestricted role assignment for snapshot authority
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: snapshot timing can be weaponized in downstream integrations

analysis_questions: Where are snapshots consumed (airdrop, governance, off-chain indexing)?
Is there an expected cadence or authorization model for snapshots?
Could a malicious snapshooter create excessive snapshots to increase storage/gas costs?
validation_requirements: Trace all uses of ERC20Snapshot in the broader codebase (outside provided scope)
Review operational playbooks for snapshooter management
Model snapshot spam/storage impact if snapshooter is malicious

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If snapshots drive an airdrop allocation, attacker obtains snapshooter, bridges/mints tokens briefly, takes snapshot, then exits position, capturing allocation unfairly.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Depends on downstream usage; could be material in governance/airdrops
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible via governance rollback, but often irreversible once executed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing integration tests around snapshot consumers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor Snapshot events and snapshooter changes

threats.title: Cryptographic Proof Bypass via Watchdog Unsuspend
threats.hypothesis_description: The `suspendMessages` function allows the `bridge_watchdog` to set `proofReceipt.receivedAt` to `block.timestamp` when unsuspending, which completely bypasses the Merkle proof verification in `processMessage`.
threats.investigation_rationale: A compromised watchdog can suspend and then unsuspend a completely fabricated message hash. Because `receivedAt != 0`, `processMessage` considers the message proven and executes it, allowing the attacker to mint arbitrary bridged tokens or drain bridge ETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = _timestamp during unsuspend bypasses _proveSignalReceived

preconditions: Attacker compromises the bridge_watchdog key

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bool isMessageProven = receivedAt != 0;
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Watchdog can artificially set receivedAt, bypassing cryptographic cross-chain security
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total drain of all bridge ETH and canonical vault tokens

economic_questions: Can this be executed atomically to prevent intervention?
validation_requirements: Test processing a fake message after suspend+unsuspend

threats.title: Irrecoverable Token Loss via Destination Address Banning
threats.hypothesis_description: If a vault address is banned on the destination chain via `banAddress`, any in-flight messages to that vault are immediately marked `DONE` by `processMessage` without invoking the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the message status is set to `DONE`, it can never be recalled on the source chain (which requires `FAILED` status). The bridged tokens are permanently burned/locked on the source chain and never minted/released on the destination chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addressBanned[_message.to] marks message DONE without invocation

preconditions: Admin bans a vault address
Users have in-flight messages targeting that vault

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _updateMessageStatus(msgHash, Status.DONE);
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin action causes legitimate messages to complete without executing token logic
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, but causes severe user fund loss

economic_questions: Is there any recovery mechanism for messages marked DONE?
validation_requirements: Test processing a vault message while the vault is banned

threats.title: Watchdog Unsuspend Bypasses Cryptographic Proof Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Unsuspending a message sets its receivedAt timestamp, which causes processMessage to skip Merkle proof verification, allowing a compromised watchdog to forge arbitrary cross-chain messages.
threats.investigation_rationale: `processMessage` uses `receivedAt != 0` to determine if a message is proven. `suspendMessages(false)` sets this to `block.timestamp`, creating a backdoor that bypasses `_proveSignalReceived` entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = _timestamp; and bool isMessageProven = receivedAt != 0;

preconditions: Watchdog role is compromised or acts maliciously to unsuspend a never-proven message hash

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: An administrative function designed for pausing inadvertently writes to the state variable used to verify cryptographic proofs.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the watchdog key is compromised.

operational_questions: Why does `suspendMessages` write to `receivedAt` instead of a dedicated `isSuspended` mapping?
Can this be exploited accidentally if the watchdog unsuspends a typo'd hash?
validation_requirements: Call `suspendMessages(false)` on a random hash, then call `processMessage` with a matching fake message. Verify it executes.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls `processMessage` with the fake message. It executes without a Merkle proof.

threats.title: ERC20Vault changeBridgedToken leaves stale bridgedToCanonical mapping for old token, enabling future logic confusion or bypass in upgrades/tools
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken attempts to delete a mapping entry but deletes bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] instead of bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]. Since _btokenNew was required to be unmapped, this deletion is a no-op and the old bridged token remains mapped to the canonical token forever while also being blacklisted. This breaks the canonical<->bridged bijection invariant and can cause future contract upgrades, off-chain indexers, or emergency tools that rely on bridgedToCanonical alone (and forget to check blacklist) to treat the old token as still valid for burn/mint accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Stale state is a frequent root cause of operational incidents during migrations/upgrades, particularly when multiple systems (indexers, risk engines, bridges on other chains) assume one-to-one mappings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]; btokenBlacklist[btokenOld_]=true; bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] not deleted

preconditions: At least one token migration performed via changeBridgedToken
A later code path/tool uses bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Migration produces inconsistent on-chain registry state across two mappings

operational_questions: Do any in-scope contracts or off-chain services rely on bridgedToCanonical for validity without checking btokenBlacklist?
Are there admin rescue/migration scripts that could be confused by stale mappings?
Do upgrades plan to iterate over mappings or assume bijection?
validation_requirements: Write a migration test: migrate token, confirm bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists
Review all usages of bridgedToCanonical and confirm blacklist is always enforced
Assess indexer and monitoring assumptions around token registry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for a token migration to occur
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Target any component that trusts bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] as authoritative
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use old token in that component to induce incorrect mint/burn/valuation actions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Depends on existence of a relying component; may require upgrade timing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Mis-accounting, stuck migrations, erroneous UI/indexer state
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; requires upgrade or explicit cleanup migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token migration operations and token registry integrity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: BridgedTokenChanged events followed by lingering bridgedToCanonical for old token
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Off-chain services showing two bridged tokens for one canonical token
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users attempting to use blacklisted btoken in unexpected flows

threats.title: SignalService is intentionally unpausable; any discovered proof-verification bug becomes non-containable at contract level
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService inherits pause/unpause but overrides _authorizePause to always revert, making pause/unpause unusable. If a critical vulnerability is discovered in proof verification (LibTrieProof / MerkleTrie / RLP parsing), the protocol cannot freeze SignalService to halt proof acceptance. Bridge/vaults may be pausable, but any other consumers (now or future) may remain exploitable, and proofs may still be cached on-chain by proveSignalReceived side effects.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational containment risk rather than an immediate exploit. Given SignalService sits at the trust boundary for all bridging proofs, inability to pause increases incident response difficulty.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

preconditions: A vulnerability exists in SignalService proof verification or related libraries
Attack can be executed without needing SignalService to be paused (likely)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No on-chain kill switch for the proof verification layer
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any incident affecting SignalService correctness

operational_questions: What is the emergency response plan if SignalService proof logic is found vulnerable?
Can pausing Bridge/vaults alone fully contain impact, considering caching side effects in proveSignalReceived?
Are there any other contracts relying on SignalService that cannot be paused?
validation_requirements: Map all SignalService consumers and their pause controls
Simulate incident response: pause Bridge/vaults and assess whether harmful caching is still possible
Review upgrade paths and timelock delays for emergency fixes
